In our agentic AI market deck, we identify pain points entrepreneurs should prioritize
OUR METHODOLOGY TO BUILD THIS TRACKER
We built this agentic AI funding tracker by reviewing every publicly disclosed equity round raised by pure-play agentic AI companies between July 2025 and June 2026. A company counts as pure-play when more than 80% of its activity is dedicated to software that plans, executes, verifies, and completes multi-step tasks through tools, integrations, context, memory, or human approval gates.
We applied four filters to build the dataset. First, we only included equity rounds, so grants, debt, and revenue financing are excluded. Second, we only counted rounds of $300K or more. Third, we only kept pure-play agentic AI companies. And fourth, every entry had to be confirmed by a direct company announcement, a press release, or a tier-1 media report, with the source URL preserved for every row.
We excluded undisclosed-amount rounds because including them would have distorted every dollar-based metric in the agentic AI market. The final dataset contains 59 disclosed deals across 58 unique companies, and every average, median, share, and concentration ratio is computed on that disclosed sample. Privately raised rounds that were never publicly announced are necessarily missing, which is a known limitation of any public-only agentic AI funding tracker.
How active has fundraising been in the agentic AI market?
As of June 2026, fundraising in the agentic AI market has been very active on deal count and uneven on dollars. Over the past 12 months, companies raised 59 disclosed equity rounds and $4.738B combined, which works out to 4.9 deals per month.
The agentic AI market is not a market with only a few visible startups. The dataset includes 58 unique companies, so almost every disclosed round represents a separate company rather than repeated fundraising from the same small group.
Dollar flow is much more volatile than deal flow. The market averaged $394.8M raised per month, but May 2026 alone reached $2.082B because Sierra and Cognition both raised very large rounds.
The monthly median is a better guide than the monthly average. The median month raised $206.1M, which is roughly half the average, showing how strongly one month distorted the 12-month picture.
If you want to go deeper on the companies behind this activity, see our market report covering agentic AI fundraising.
How concentrated has fundraising been in the agentic AI market?
As of June 2026, fundraising in the agentic AI market is highly concentrated at the top. Over the past 12 months, the single largest deal accounts for 21.1% of all capital, the top 3 deals reach 48.5%, and the top 5 reach 59.9%.
The largest rounds do not merely add to the market total. They define the market total. Cognition’s $1.0B round, Sierra’s $950M round, and Sierra’s earlier $350M round together represent almost half of disclosed capital.
The same concentration appears in categories. Vertical AI Agents capture 55.7% of disclosed capital, while Agentic AI Applications capture another 30.1%. Together, those two categories hold 85.8% of the agentic AI market’s funding.
This means aggregate funding headlines should be read carefully. The agentic AI market is active, but the dollar story is mostly a story about a few companies that investors already believe can become category leaders.
How much of the agentic AI funding signal is driven by outliers?
As of June 2026, a large share of the agentic AI funding signal is driven by outliers. Over the past 12 months, only 13 deals exceeded $50M, but those rounds account for most of the capital raised.
Excluding rounds above $50M reduces disclosed capital from $4.738B to $1.038B. That is a 78.1% reduction, which means the market’s apparent capital abundance depends overwhelmingly on megarounds.
The average round size is $80.3M, while the median round size is $30.0M. That gap matters because the average describes the headline market, while the median better describes ordinary financing conditions.
The top 10 deals hold 73.3% of all disclosed capital. So when reading any agentic AI funding total, the first question should be whether the number reflects broad market health or a small set of winner-financing events.
This chart, included in our agentic AI market deck, shows how Cognition is positioned in agentic AI
Is the agentic AI market broad with many targets, or narrow with few fundable companies?
As of June 2026, the agentic AI market is broad on company count but narrow on late-stage conviction. Over the past 12 months, 58 unique companies raised 59 disclosed rounds, which shows wide formation across the market.
The breadth is strongest at Seed and Series A. Those two stages account for 41 of 59 disclosed deals, or 69.5% of activity, which means the median agentic AI company is still being formed or recently validated.
But the dollar pool is not broad. Series D+, Growth Equity, and Series B rounds absorb most of the disclosed capital, and the top 10 deals hold 73.3% of dollars.
So the agentic AI market has many targets, but only a small set has crossed into true winner-financing territory. That is the core tension in the funding data.
Is agentic AI mostly an early-stage formation market or a late-stage scaling market?
As of June 2026, the agentic AI market is early-stage by deal count but late-stage by capital. Over the past 12 months, Seed and Series A represent 69.5% of deals, but only 24.5% of disclosed capital.
Seed rounds are numerous but relatively small. The dataset includes 22 Seed deals, with a median Seed round of about $9.2M and a total of $292.6M.
Late-stage dollars dominate the market’s narrative. Series D+ alone represents $2.300B, or 48.5% of disclosed capital, from only 3 deals.
This creates a split-screen market. New agentic AI companies are forming quickly, but investor conviction is concentrating around companies with proof of execution, budget ownership, and enterprise-scale distribution.
We cover this stage split in more detail in our deeper analysis of the agentic AI market.

title_original: SAP Unveils the Autonomous Enterprise | SAP Sapphire - SAP News

Press Room
SAP Unveils the Autonomous Enterprise
Press Release
by SAP News
May 12, 2026
The company introduces a unified SAP Business AI Platform, deepening partnerships with Anthropic, Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, Microsoft, NVIDIA and Palantir
ORLANDO — At SAP Sapphire in 2026, SAP SE (NYSE: SAP) introduced the Autonomous Enterprise to help enhance the world’s most critical business workflows, so that humans and AI work together to meet the accelerating demands of global business profitably, strategically and safely.
SAP Sapphire in 2026: Advancing the Autonomous Enterprise
Read the innovation news guide
“For the mission-critical processes of our customers, ‘almost right’ just isn’t good enough,” said Christian Klein, CEO of SAP SE. “By uniting SAP Business AI Platform with SAP Autonomous Suite, we anchor AI agents in the business processes, data and governance so they can deliver accurate, compliant and secure outcomes, unlocking new sources of revenue and meaningful cost savings.”
The Autonomous Enterprise includes a unified AI platform for building, contextualizing and governing agents, an autonomous suite that executes core business operations and a new user experience that redefines how people work with enterprise software.
Introducing SAP Business AI Platform
SAP Business AI Platform is a new foundation for building and deploying enterprise AI grounded in real business context. SAP Business AI Platform now unifies SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Business AI into a single, governed environment.
At its core is the SAP Knowledge Graph solution, which gives AI agents a structured map of business entities, processes and relationships across a customer’s SAP landscape. Joule Studio is SAP’s AI-first solution for building enterprise agents, applications and agentic workflows. Developers can build using the no-code, pro-code and AI frameworks of their choice on SAP-managed infrastructure that is secure, scalable and optimized for enterprise AI.
Deploying SAP Autonomous Suite Across Every Business Function and Industry
Building on this foundation, SAP also introduced SAP Autonomous Suite, which enables SAP’s existing business applications with AI agents capable of running processes from start-to-finish.
The suite will deploy more than 50 domain-specific Joule Assistants across finance, supply chain, procurement, human capital management and customer experience. These assistants will automate end-to-end processes by orchestrating a subset of over 200 specialized agents to execute precise tasks. For example, the new Autonomous Close Assistant can compress the financial close process from weeks to days by automating journal entries, reconciliation and error resolution across the entire process.
SAP also launched Industry AI, expanding its deep industry portfolio through seven autonomous solutions that will enable start-to-finish industry processes and embed sector-specific process logic, data models and regulatory requirements. At SAP Sapphire, SAP showcased its work with European energy giant RWE to leverage Industry AI, helping reduce unplanned downtime across its offshore wind turbines. With SAP’s Autonomous Asset Management scenario, AI agents are designed to analyze data from thousands of past incidents, identify the likely root cause and generate pre-filled work orders with the right tools and proven fixes from other sites.
Designing the Autonomous User Experience
The company also revealed Joule Work, redefining how users engage with SAP software. Instead of navigating individual applications and entering data across several screens, users will now interact primarily with Joule. By describing a desired business outcome, Joule will orchestrate the right combination of workflows, data and agents to get it done.
Joule Work goes beyond conversation, proactively surfacing relevant insights and automating routine tasks behind the scenes so work moves forward even when humans aren’t actively steering it. It will be available on desktop, mobile and voice across SAP and non-SAP systems.
Accelerating the Customer Journey Toward Autonomy with €100 Million Infusion
SAP evolved its customer and partner programs to help accelerate the organization’s journey to the Autonomous Enterprise. To catalyze adoption, the company has launched a €100 million fund for SAP partners to help customers deploy SAP-built AI assistants and agents. The fund is also available to partners that extend or build new partner agents on the new SAP Business AI Platform using Joule Studio.
SAP has enhanced its RISE with SAP and SAP GROW offerings to accelerate AI adoption. Both include access to the Joule Assistants portfolio; RISE with SAP customers will have three assistants activated within their first year, while SAP GROW customers receive full portfolio access at onboarding. SAP S/4HANA on-premises and SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) customers are not excluded: those that commit to transitioning the majority of their current landscape to SAP Cloud ERP gain access to select AI scenarios, bridging the gap between their current landscape and their cloud destination
SAP also introduced new agent-led transformation tooling that can reduce ERP migration efforts by more than 35 percent, driving faster and more predictable projects by automating system analysis, code remediation, configuration and testing at scale.
Lastly, SAP announced a full slate of strategic partnerships across each category:
Platform and suite partnerships include Anthropic , with Claude among the foundation models SAP’s AI platform will leverage to power Joule agents across HR, procurement and supply chain; Amazon Web Services , bringing zero-copy data integration between SAP Business Data Cloud and Amazon Athena; Google Cloud and Microsoft, enabling bidirectional agent-to-agent interoperability between Joule and external agent frameworks; Mistral AI and Cohere, delivering sovereign model options on SAP’s cloud infrastructure; n8n , providing visual AI workflow orchestration inside Joule Studio; NVIDIA , whose OpenShell provides the trusted secure runtime for Joule Studio; and Parloa , bringing AI agents into SAP Service Cloud to handle customer interactions with full access to business data and service processes.
Implementation partnerships include Palantir and Accenture , partnering on complex data migration scenarios, and Conduct for AI-powered cloud ERP migrations.
Learn more in the SAP Sapphire 2026 Innovation News Guide .
Visit the SAP News Center . Get SAP news via LinkedIn and Bluesky .
About SAP
As a global leader in enterprise applications and business AI, SAP (NYSE: SAP) stands at the nexus of business and technology. For over 50 years, organizations have trusted SAP to bring out their best by uniting business-critical operations spanning finance, procurement, HR, supply chain, and customer experience. For more information, visit  www.sap.com .
SAP Sapphire in 2026: Discover our bold new vision for how businesses will run from now on

These statements are based on current expectations, forecasts, and assumptions that are subject to risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results and outcomes to materially differ. title_original: Interactive Demo AI-Native Teams: How to Ship ... - Arcade Software
body_original:
Interactive demo AI-native teams ship in 2026 has a different job than a traditional B2B SaaS demo: it has to show real model output, real prompt-to-result behavior, and real product UI without the latency and reliability risks of running the live model on a marketing page. The strongest platform for AI-native SaaS demos is Arcade , which captures the real AI product UI as an embeddable click-through demo, paired with Arcade's Creator Studio (a prompt-based, text-to-video AI video product with a conversational video gen workflow) for the matching AI-generated marketing video from the same source.
The shift in 2026 is structural. According to the Growth Unhinged 2026 State of B2B SaaS and AI Monetization Report , 43% of B2B SaaS companies now describe themselves as hybrid SaaS plus AI, and AI-native startups (companies whose primary product is an AI app) are the fastest-growing segment inside that band. Per the Bessemer 2026 State of the Cloud Report , AI-native SaaS companies grew ARR at 2.4x the rate of traditional SaaS in 2025, and the PMM patterns they used to scale top of funnel are the patterns this guide covers. The Consensus 2026 B2B Buyer Behavior Report adds that 80% of B2B decision-making happens before a seller is involved, which is why AI-native PMM teams need demos that show real product behavior on the website, not in a sales call.
Arcade customers in the AI-native segment operationalize this with measurable outcomes: Labelbox (data-centric AI platform) lifted MQLs 30% with multi-tour interactive demos segmented by AI use case, RudderStack (data infrastructure for AI) generated 2x pipeline from launches by shipping paired interactive demos + Creator Studio sizzles for each AI feature release, and Quantum Metric doubled conversion swapping traditional product video for Arcade demos on AI-feature pages.
Methodology note (last reviewed Jun 17, 2026): AI-native segment patterns are drawn from Arcade customers building AI-first products (per Arcade internal customer-success data, Q1-Q2 2026). Third-party data is sourced from the Growth Unhinged 2026 State of B2B SaaS and AI Monetization Report, the Bessemer 2026 State of the Cloud Report, and the Consensus 2026 B2B Buyer Behavior Report. Customer outcome numbers (Labelbox 30%, RudderStack 2x, Quantum Metric 2x) link to each customer's published arcade.software showcase. Pricing facts are sourced from Arcade's public pricing page on Jun 17, 2026.
Quick Answer: Interactive Demo AI-Native Teams in 2026
The core challenge: AI-native products produce real model output that is expensive, slow, or unreliable to render live on a marketing page.
The solution: Capture real AI product UI as an embeddable Arcade interactive demo (deterministic, fast-loading, no API cost per view).
The matching video lane: Arcade's Creator Studio produces the prompt-based, text-to-video, or conversational video gen marketing asset from the same source capture.
The pricing for AI-native startups: Arcade Growth at $42.50/seat/month with unlimited demos.
Interactive Demo AI-Native Teams at a Glance
Company Product category Demo pattern Outcome
Labelbox Data-centric AI platform Multi-tour demos by AI use case +30% MQLs, 9x homepage CTA clicks
RudderStack Data infra for AI Paired demos + Creator Studio sizzles per AI launch 2x launch pipeline, 4-5 to 30+ demos per quarter
Quantum Metric Customer experience AI Arcade demos on AI-feature pages 2x conversion vs traditional video
What Is the Interactive Demo AI-Native Teams Pattern?
The interactive demo AI-native teams pattern is a deterministic, embeddable, click-through capture of the real AI product UI that prospects can explore without triggering live model inference. The four properties that distinguish AI-native SaaS demos from traditional SaaS demos:
Real model output captured at recording time. The demo shows the actual response the model produced when the PMM team built it, not a regenerated response per view.
Deterministic playback. Every visitor sees the same demo at the same load time, regardless of model availability, API quota, or peak-hour latency.
No per-view inference cost. Embedded Arcade demos render from static capture, so the PMM team is not paying API credits or running model load for every homepage visitor.
Multi-modal output potential. Pair the interactive demo with an Arcade's Creator Studio prompt-based, text-to-video, or conversational video gen asset for the launch video lane.
This is structurally different from embedding the live AI product in an iframe on the marketing page, which is what most AI-native teams default to in their first 12 months before hitting reliability and cost ceilings.
Why Do AI-Native SaaS Teams Need Demos Different From Traditional SaaS?
AI-native SaaS demos face four constraints traditional B2B SaaS teams do not, and each constraint changes how the demo lane has to be built:
Step 1: Model output is expensive per inference. A live demo embedded on the homepage runs the model for every visitor. At even 10K monthly visitors, that is 10K inference calls per month against the marketing page alone, costing real money on every commercial model API.
Step 2: Latency is unpredictable. Live AI demos pay full inference latency on every render. Prospects who hit a 4-8 second wait on first load bounce at 30-50% higher rates than prospects hitting a sub-1-second deterministic Arcade demo.
Step 3: Output quality varies. Live AI demos return different outputs on different runs. PMM teams cannot guarantee the demo shows the "best" response, which means the homepage can show a worse-than-typical model day to a high-intent visitor.
Step 4: API outages are visible to prospects. Live AI demos break when the upstream model API has an incident. Arcade demos do not.
The pattern AI-native PMM teams settle on is to capture the strongest model output once, ship it as an Arcade interactive demo, and pair it with Creator Studio video for the AI feature launch lane.
How Do AI-Native PMM Teams Ship Product Demos for AI Apps in 2026?
The strongest product demos for AI apps in 2026 follow a consistent 4-step pattern:
Step 1: Capture the strongest model output. Run the AI feature 5-10 times during demo build, pick the response that best represents the product, and capture the full prompt-to-output flow in Arcade.
Step 2: Layer in Avery AI narration and callouts. The Arcade editor adds hotspots and Avery voiceover from the captured product flow, so the demo explains what the model is doing without requiring a sales rep.
Step 3: Embed on the AI feature page and pricing page. Replace any live-model iframe with the Arcade embed. Page load time drops to sub-1-second, and inference cost per visitor drops to zero.
Step 4: Ship the paired Creator Studio video. Arcade's Creator Studio (prompt-based, text-to-video, conversational video gen) generates the matching 30-60 second AI-generated marketing video from the same source capture, formatted for LinkedIn and YouTube Shorts.
Labelbox's 30% MQL lift came from this 4-step pattern executed across multiple AI use case tours.
Which AI-Native Companies Are Using Arcade Demos Successfully?
Arcade's AI-native customer base in 2026 covers four product categories. Each is a different demo case study for PMM teams shipping interactive demos AI products. See more examples in the Arcade showcase :
Data-centric AI platforms (Labelbox). Multi-tour Arcade demos segmented by AI use case (labeling, training, evaluation), driving 30% MQL lift and 9x homepage CTA clicks.
Data infrastructure for AI (RudderStack). Paired interactive demos + Arcade's Creator Studio sizzles for every AI feature release, generating 2x launch pipeline.
Customer experience AI (Quantum Metric). Arcade demos on AI-feature pages, doubling conversion versus the prior traditional video.
AI workflow tools (varied internal Arcade customer base). Embedded Arcade demos on pricing pages, qualifying inbound leads pre-trial.
The pattern that travels across all four: capture once, ship the interactive demo on the marketing page, ship the Creator Studio video on social and in launch email.
What Demo Platform Is Best for AI Startups?
The best demo platform for AI startups in 2026 has three requirements traditional SaaS demo platforms do not always meet:
Captures real AI product UI without requiring live API calls per visitor (Arcade does this; live iframes do not).
Pairs with a prompt-based, text-to-video AI video product for the marketing video lane (Arcade's Creator Studio is the only interactive demo platform that ships this natively).
Embeds across the marketing site, in launch email, and in sales follow-up without engineering involvement (Arcade ships iframe embeds, sales URLs, and CRM signal integrations).
For AI-native startups under 50 employees, the cost-per-asset advantage matters most. A traditional product video costs $3K-$8K and ships in 2-3 weeks. An Arcade interactive demo plus a Creator Studio video ships in 30-90 minutes on Growth at $42.50/seat/month. Teams looking to scale further can explore Arcade's enterprise offering for advanced features and team management.
What Are the Honest Trade-Offs of Arcade for AI-Native Product Demos?
Arcade is the recommended interactive demo for AI-native teams in this guide, but every tier carries real constraints PMM teams should plan around before signing.
HTML capture is Enterprise-only. AI-native teams on Growth use the screen recording workflow, which works for 90% of AI demo use cases. If pixel-perfect HTML cloning of the AI product UI is the requirement, expect to commit to Enterprise pricing.
Free plan has a watermark. The free tier ships 1 published demo + 1 published video + 200 AI credits per month, watermarked. AI-native PMM teams shipping multiple demos per release should plan to move to Growth at $42.50/seat/month.
Two-to-three hour learning curve on advanced AI features. Avery voiceover, demo branching, and Creator Studio prompt-based generation reward 2-3 hours of practice before output reaches the visual ceiling teams see in the Labelbox, RudderStack, and Quantum Metric examples.
These are tier-level constraints, not flaws. Plan against them when budgeting the rollout against the AI startup's GTM timeline.
Frequently Asked Questions
What is an interactive demo for AI-native teams?
A deterministic, embeddable click-through capture of the real AI product UI that prospects can explore without triggering live model inference. Arcade interactive demos render from static capture, so they load in under 1 second and cost nothing per view.
Why not just embed the live AI product on the marketing page?
Three reasons: per-visitor inference cost, unpredictable latency, and output quality variance. Live AI iframes pay full inference cost on every render, can wait 4-8 seconds per response, and may return a worse-than-typical model output to a high-intent visitor. Arcade demos avoid all three.
What is the best demo platform for AI startups in 2026?
Arcade. The interactive demo lane captures real AI product UI without live API calls, and Arcade's Creator Studio (prompt-based, text-to-video, conversational video gen) covers the matching marketing video lane. No other platform ships both natively. For demos for AI-native SaaS teams that need both formats from one source, this is the structural advantage.
How do AI-native PMM teams use interactive demos AI products on the marketing site?
Capture once (the strongest model output), embed on the AI feature page and pricing page, pair with a Creator Studio video for launch and social. Labelbox's 30% MQL lift came from this exact pattern executed across multiple AI use case tours.
Can interactive demos for AI products replace a live trial?
For top-of-funnel and consideration-stage content, yes. For decision-stage trial activation, the interactive demo is the warm-up, not the replacement. AI-native teams typically pair the marketing-page Arcade demo with a sandboxed trial environment for hands-on evaluation.
What is the difference between product demos for AI apps and traditional SaaS demos?
AI demos need to show real model output without paying live inference cost per visitor. Traditional SaaS demos show product UI that does not have per-visitor compute cost. Arcade handles both, but the AI-native lane benefits more from the deterministic-capture pattern.
Arcade
June 29, 2026
Share on

title_original: Top 10 Funding Rounds: AI, LegalTech, HealthTech, Robotics ...
body_original:
Top 10 Pre-seed to Series A funding rounds of the week:
Top 10 Pre-seed to Series A funding rounds of the week:
⚫ AI ⚫
🔶 1. Jedify, a platform that builds context graphs that help AI agents understand a company's data, permissions, workflows, and terminology, raised a $24M Series A round.
→ Investors: Norwest, Snowflake Ventures & others
→ Founded by: Assaf Henkin, Adi Elimelech, Erik Shani
🔶 2. Niteshift, a platform that provides cloud development environments where AI coding agents can run, test, and verify software, raised a $7M seed round.
→ Investors: Greylock, Amplify Partners, Lux Capital, BoxGroup, SV Angel
→ Founded by: Sajid Mehmood, Conor Branagan
🔶 3. Concentrate AI a platform that routes AI tasks across different models so developers can control token costs, raised a $5M pre-seed round.
→ Investors: True Ventures, RRE Ventures
→ Founded by: Ari Jacoby, Todd Lieberman, Zach Moskow
🔶 4. RELAI, a platform that analyzes AI agent failures and feedback to identify root causes and update prompts, workflows, and memory, raised a $5.4M pre-seed round.
→ Investors: .406 Ventures, AI Tinkerers Fund
→ Founded by: Soheil Feizi
⚫ LegalTech ⚫
🔶 5. Fearn, a platform that automates patent drafting, figure generation, and compliance workflows to help engineers and scientists produce patent filings faster and at lower cost, raised a $5.5M seed round.
→ Investors: Kindred Ventures a16z speedrun & others
→ Founded by: Han Kim, Angela Gao
🔶 6. Billables AI, a platform that automates time tracking and analyzes work activity data to generate billing records and operational insights for law firms, raised a $10.2M Series A round.
→ Investors: Avenue Growth Partner & others
→ Founded by: Arvind Sujeeth, Nancy Jeng
⚫ HealthTech ⚫
🔶 7. Uncovr, a platform that analyzes surgical and endoscopic video to generate operative reports and procedural billing codes for surgeons and hospitals, raised a $7M seed round.
→ Investors: Index Ventures & others
→ Founded by: Ines Iraki, Johann Diep, Eric Vibert, MD, PhD
⚫ Robotics ⚫
🔶 8. Kyber, a company developing real-time infrastructure for controlling robots, drones, and autonomous systems remotely, raised a $5M seed round.
→ Investors: Lightspeed Venture Partners & others
→ Founded by: Jean-Baptiste Kempf
⚫ FinTech ⚫
🔶 9. Capsa AI, a platform that organizes, searches, and analyzes investment data for private capital firms, raised an $18M Series A round.
→ Investors: TX Ventures & others
→ Founded by: Danyal Özdüzenciler, Callum Downie
⚫ Consumer ⚫
🔶 10. Odessia Travel, an AI-powered travel agent that allows users to plan and book entire trips in a single conversation, raised a $6M round.
→ Investors: Sequoia
→ Founded by: Francis Davidson
Huge congrats to everyone here! 🥂
PS: Track new VC fund announcements + full details here: https://lnkd.in/d7X8up9h
PPS: Join the weekly EverythingStartups newsletter for more on where early-stage capital is flowing across USA, Europe, and Israel: https://lnkd.in/dyNtt9s4

title_original: Sector Snapshot: Robotics Startups On Fire As Venture Funding ...
body_original:
Unicorn Board Tech Layoffs Tracker Billion-Dollar Exits Largest Funding Deals Tracker Web3 Tracker Venture funding reports
Q1 2023
Q1 2023 Global Q1 2023 North America Q1 2023 Europe Q1 2023 Latin America Q1 2023 Asia
2022
2022 Global 2022 North America 2022 Europe 2022 Latin America 2022 Asia
Mary Ann Azevedo
Shares
Email
Facebook
Twitter
LinkedIn
Robotics startup funding hit a record high in 2025, per Crunchbase data . And that trend is continuing in 2026 so far, with funding to the sector already eclipsing 2025’s totals.
Globally, robotics startups have so far raised $18.8 billion in 2026, compared to $15 billion in the full year of 2025. The figure also handily surpasses the $14.1 billion raised in the peak venture funding year of 2021, and we still have more than six months of fundraising left.
The impressive rise in funding reflects a marked shift in perception among venture investors about the robotics sector, which was traditionally considered an expensive, asset-heavy hardware gamble. In particular, investors appear to be drawn to startups working on embodied AI, or artificial intelligence with a physical body that interacts with the real world in real time.
Noteworthy recent rounds
The surge in funding is driven by a number of robotics-focused startups raising considerable capital from investors this year. Also, interestingly, two of the five largest raises in 2026 to date have been by Austin-based companies .
Topping the list of largest deals in 2026 so far is Austin-based Saronic , a defense tech startup focused on autonomous sea vessels. In March, the 4-year-old company raised $1.75 billion in Series D funding, bringing its total funding to around $2.6 billion. Kleiner Perkins led the round, which set Saronic’s valuation at $9.25 billion — more than double its Series C level in 2025.
Earlier this month, Germany’s Neura Robotics , a developer of AI infrastructure for robots to learn, collaborate and operate across real-world environments, said it secured up to $1.4 billion in Series C funding. Tether led that raise.
In January, Skild AI , a robotics company building an “omni-bodied” brain to operate any robot for any task, announced that it had raised $1.4 billion , tripling its valuation to over $14 billion. That financing came just over seven months after Skild raised a $135 million Series B at a $4.5 billion valuation. SoftBank Group led the startup’s latest round, which included participation from NVentures , Nvidia ’s venture capital arm.
On June 15, Beijing-based Shihang Intelligent , which creates water robots and intelligent unmanned equipment, raised $1 billion in a massive Series A round led by Beijing Shanghe Momentum Private Equity Fund .
And in February, AI-powered robotics company Apptronik raised $520 million in an extension of its $415 million Series A raise in February 2025, bringing the total round to over $935 million. Existing backers B Capital , Google , Mercedes-Benz and Peak6 joined new investors, including AT&T Ventures and manufacturing giant John Deere in participating in the extension.
Interestingly, Rivian spinout Mind Robotics has already raised two rounds in 2026. In March, the Palo Alto, California-based startup closed on a $500 million Series A round, co-led by Accel and Andreessen Horowitz . Then in May, it raised another $400 million in a financing led by Kleiner Perkins . The company is developing an AI-enabled industrial robotics platform focused on automating industrial and manufacturing tasks at scale.
Exits
While mergers and acquisitions have been relatively robust with several strategic buyouts, the robotics IPO landscape is a bit quieter, particularly in the U.S.
In China, however, a number of robotics companies have recently gone public. The highly anticipated public listing of Unitree Robotics , targeting a $3 billion to $7 billion valuation, was considered a milestone for the industry. In March, the company filed for an initial public offering to list on the Shanghai Stock Exchange , and its IPO was widely expected to spur other startups in the space to pursue their own public-market debuts.
Robotphoenix , a startup based in China’s Shandong province that makes lightweight industrial robots, in May listed on the HKEX , raising about $86 million. And it did not disappoint. Robotphoenix closed its first full day of trading at HK$53.75 ($6.86 U.S.), up nearly 80%, though shares have dipped to the HK$37 range more recently.
On the M&A front, a number of Big Tech and automotive giants have been aggressively acquiring embodied AI and humanoid talent to anchor their physical automation strategies.
In February, AI-powered supply chain provider Symbotic acquired Fox Robotics , an Austin-based maker of autonomous forklifts and lift trucks.
Skild AI said in April that it had picked up the robotics arm of Zebra Technologies in an effort to deploy its technology to warehouses.
And in May, tech giant Meta entered the humanoid robotics field directly by acquiring San Diego-based Assured Robot Intelligence . The team was absorbed into Meta’s Superintelligence Labs unit to accelerate training of its foundational physical AI model.
Related Crunchbase queries:
Global Robotics Venture Funding In 2026
Largest Robotics-Related Rounds Of 2026
Global Physical AI Venture Funding In 2026
Related reading:
5 Startup Sectors Seeing Big Funding Growth
The Year Of Humanoid Robots
Embodied AI Fuels Record Robotics Funding In China As IPO Momentum Builds
Amid Record Robotics Funding, Apptronik Raises $520M Series A Extension To Boost Production Of Humanoid Robot Apollo
Robotics Startup Skild AI Lands $1.4B, Tripling Valuation To $14B In Just 7 Months
Illustration: Dom Guzman
Tags unicorn

Crunchbase Daily.

title_original: Q1 2026 Venture Capital Hits $297B: AI Captures 81 ... - Tech Insider
body_original:
Nadia Dubois
April 19, 2026
14 min read
Global venture capital investment hit $297 billion in the first quarter of 2026, obliterating every prior record and marking the most concentrated capital deployment in startup history. According to Crunchbase data published on April 1, four of the five largest venture rounds ever recorded closed in a single 90-day window, with frontier AI labs capturing nearly two-thirds of every dollar invested. The quarter reshaped the power dynamics of Silicon Valley, elevated sovereign wealth funds to kingmaker status, and left a growing number of non-AI startups struggling to compete for attention and capital.
The numbers are staggering by any measure. Investors poured capital into approximately 6,000 startups worldwide, up more than 150% from the $118 billion raised in Q4 2025, according to TechCrunch and Crunchbase reporting. The United States absorbed $250 billion of the total, representing 81% of global venture funding, up from 55% a year earlier.[1][2] The concentration raises fundamental questions about whether the venture capital industry is funding a genuine technological revolution or inflating the largest asset bubble in financial history.
Google · Preferred Sources
Don't miss new tech stories on Google
Add Tech Insider once in the Google app and our stories appear in your news suggestions.
Add Now
The Four Mega-Rounds That Rewrote Venture Capital History
The Q1 2026 record rests almost entirely on four extraordinary funding rounds that collectively totaled $188 billion, accounting for roughly 63% of all global venture investment in the quarter. OpenAI’s $122 billion raise, the largest private funding round in history, valued the company at $852 billion and attracted a consortium of corporate and institutional investors. Amazon contributed approximately $50 billion, Nvidia $30 billion, and SoftBank $30 billion, with additional participation from Microsoft, Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia Capital, Thrive Capital, Temasek, and BlackRock.
Anthropic followed with a $30 billion Series G round that pushed its valuation to $380 billion. Singapore’s GIC and Coatue Management led the round, with participation from D.E. Shaw, Founders Fund, Microsoft, Nvidia, and the Qatar Investment Authority. Elon Musk’s xAI closed a $20 billion Series E, with Andreessen Horowitz leading alongside 8VC, Lightspeed Venture Partners, and Shield Capital. Waymo secured $16 billion from a group that included Lightspeed, SoftBank, Amazon, Jeff Bezos, and Coatue.
“The first quarter of 2026 didn’t just set a record – it shattered every benchmark venture capital has ever known,” noted analysis from Intellizence . “For the first time in history, a single funding round exceeded the entire prior quarterly record for global startup investment.” The OpenAI round alone surpassed the total venture capital deployed in the entire United States during most quarters of 2024.
Top 10 Largest Startup Funding Rounds in Q1 2026
Company Amount Raised Valuation Sector Lead Investors
OpenAI $122 billion $852 billion Generative AI Amazon, Nvidia, SoftBank
Anthropic $30 billion $380 billion Enterprise / Safety AI GIC, Coatue
xAI $20 billion Not disclosed Frontier AI Andreessen Horowitz
Waymo $16 billion Not disclosed Autonomous Vehicles Lightspeed, SoftBank
Databricks $5 billion $62 billion Data Infrastructure Thrive Capital
Figure AI $3.2 billion+ Not disclosed Robotics / AI Multiple investors
Shield AI $2 billion $12.7 billion Defense AI U.S. defense investors
CoreWeave $1.1 billion Not disclosed AI Cloud Infrastructure Multiple investors
FluidStack $1 billion $18 billion Neocloud / GPU Anthropic-backed
World Labs $1 billion Not disclosed Spatial Intelligence Institutional investors
The table above illustrates a defining feature of Q1 2026: the near-total domination of AI and AI-adjacent companies in mega-round fundraising. Every company in the top 10 either builds foundation models, provides AI infrastructure, or deploys AI in physical systems like autonomous vehicles and military drones. Traditional software, fintech, and consumer technology startups were almost entirely absent from the billion-dollar round club.
AI Captures 81% of All Venture Capital Dollars
The most striking statistic from Q1 2026 is the degree to which artificial intelligence has consumed the venture capital market. According to analysis from Trending Topics EU , AI companies absorbed 81% of all venture funding deployed in the quarter, totaling more than $240 billion. This represents the highest concentration of capital in a single technology sector in venture capital history, surpassing the dot-com era’s peak concentration in internet companies during Q1 2000.
The AI funding surge breaks down into two distinct categories. Frontier AI labs – companies building large language models and foundation models – captured the largest share, led by OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI. AI infrastructure companies, including GPU cloud providers like CoreWeave and FluidStack, data platforms like Databricks, and chip makers like Cerebras Systems, absorbed a significant secondary share. Application-layer AI startups, those building products on top of foundation models, received comparatively modest funding.
“What we’re seeing is a capital structure that looks more like the oil industry in the early 20th century than traditional venture capital,” said Sarah Chen, managing partner at Sapphire Ventures, in an interview with TechCrunch. “A handful of infrastructure players are absorbing almost all the capital, and everyone else is building on top of their platforms. The question is whether the returns will justify the concentration.”
Geographic Concentration: The U.S. Absorbs 83% of Global VC
The geographic distribution of Q1 2026 venture capital reveals an increasingly lopsided global landscape. The United States attracted $250 billion of the $297 billion total, representing 81% of global venture investment, up sharply from 55% in Q1 2025 according to Crunchbase data.[1][2] This concentration is almost entirely driven by the mega-rounds at U.S.-headquartered AI companies, all of which are based in San Francisco or the broader Bay Area.
China attracted $16.1 billion in Q1 2026, maintaining its position as the second-largest venture market despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and export controls on advanced AI chips. The United Kingdom drew $7.4 billion, buoyed by autonomous driving startup Wayve and continued growth in London’s fintech sector. According to KPMG’s Venture Pulse Q1 2026 report, Asia as a whole saw $33.6 billion in funding, with at least two $1 billion-plus rounds closing outside the United States.
“We are watching the largest wealth creation event in human history unfold almost entirely in one country,” said Dr. Thomas Ramge, a technology analyst at the Bertelsmann Foundation, in comments to the Financial Times. “Europe’s regulatory approach has created stability but not the kind of risk appetite that produces $100 billion funding rounds.”
Late-Stage Funding Surges 205% as Mega-Deals Dominate
The stage breakdown of Q1 2026 venture funding reveals extreme concentration at the late-stage end of the spectrum. Late-stage rounds accounted for $246.6 billion, or roughly 82% of all venture capital deployed, representing a 205% year-over-year increase according to Capitaly VC and Crunchbase data. Of this, $235 billion came from just 158 rounds of $100 million or more, meaning that fewer than 3% of all venture deals accounted for more than 79% of all capital.
Seed-stage funding showed healthier dynamics, rising 31% year-over-year to $12 billion. However, the number of seed deals fell 30% to approximately 3,800, indicating that while investors are writing larger seed checks, they are funding fewer companies. The average seed round size increased accordingly, a trend that benefits well-connected founders with strong networks but makes it harder for first-time founders without Silicon Valley connections to raise initial capital.
“The venture market has essentially bifurcated,” said Mike Volpi, general partner at Index Ventures, in an interview with Bloomberg. “You have a handful of companies raising rounds that look more like sovereign debt issuances, and then you have everyone else competing for a shrinking pool of capital. The middle has been hollowed out.”
Q1 2026 Venture Funding vs. Historical Quarters
Quarter Global VC Funding YoY Change Deal Count AI Share U.S. Share
Q1 2026 $297 billion +150% ~6,000 81% 83%
Q4 2025 $118 billion +22% ~7,200 52% 71%
Q3 2025 $97 billion +18% ~7,500 48% 68%
Q2 2025 $91 billion +15% ~7,800 44% 66%
Q1 2025 $85 billion +12% ~8,100 40% 71%
Q1 2022 (prior peak) $144 billion – ~10,500 18% 55%
The table above highlights two critical trends. First, Q1 2026 more than doubled the previous quarterly record set in Q1 2022 during the zero-interest-rate venture boom. Second, the number of deals actually declined relative to 2022 levels, meaning the record was driven entirely by larger round sizes rather than broader startup ecosystem growth. This concentration pattern has significant implications for startup diversity and innovation breadth.
Sovereign Wealth Funds Emerge as AI Kingmakers
One of the most significant structural shifts in Q1 2026 was the rise of sovereign wealth funds as decisive players in frontier AI funding. Singapore’s GIC co-led Anthropic’s $30 billion round. Temasek, another Singaporean sovereign fund, participated in OpenAI’s $122 billion raise. The Qatar Investment Authority backed Anthropic. Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund and Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala Investment Company have both increased their AI allocations substantially throughout 2025 and into 2026.
The sovereign fund involvement reflects a fundamental shift in how frontier AI development is financed. Traditional venture capital firms, even the largest, lack the balance sheet capacity to write $10 billion or $30 billion checks. Sovereign wealth funds, with combined assets exceeding $12 trillion globally, can deploy capital at this scale without the liquidity constraints that limit pension funds and university endowments.
“Sovereign wealth funds are now the most important investors in the AI ecosystem,” said Aswath Damodaran, professor of finance at New York University’s Stern School of Business. “They bring patient capital, geopolitical motivations, and balance sheets that dwarf anything a traditional VC firm can muster. The question is whether their involvement adds governance value or simply inflates valuations.”
The Valuation Question: $852 Billion for a Company With $11 Billion in Revenue
The most contentious aspect of Q1 2026’s venture capital surge is the valuation multiples being assigned to frontier AI companies. OpenAI’s $852 billion valuation comes on approximately $11.6 billion in annualized revenue, implying a revenue multiple of roughly 73x. Anthropic’s $380 billion valuation on significantly lower revenue implies an even higher multiple. By comparison, the most richly valued public technology companies typically trade at 15-25x revenue.
Defenders of these valuations argue that frontier AI companies are building infrastructure that will underpin the entire economy, similar to how cloud computing platforms generated returns that justified early investments in AWS and Azure. The bull case rests on the assumption that AI will capture a significant share of global GDP, currently estimated at $105 trillion, making even an $852 billion valuation modest relative to the total addressable market.
Critics counter that the valuations reflect greater-fool dynamics rather than fundamental analysis. “We have never seen this level of capital concentration in pre-profit companies in any industry, ever,” said Scott Galloway, professor of marketing at NYU Stern. “The implicit assumption is that these companies will achieve margins and scale that exceed anything in economic history. That may happen, but the price already reflects a perfect outcome.”
Non-AI Startups Face a Capital Drought
While AI companies bathed in record capital, the rest of the startup ecosystem experienced what founders are calling a “capital drought.” With 81% of all venture dollars flowing to AI, the remaining $57 billion was split among thousands of fintech, biotech, climate tech, enterprise SaaS, and consumer startups. Adjusted for inflation, non-AI venture funding in Q1 2026 was actually below Q1 2020 levels.
The impact is already visible in hiring data, runway extensions, and pivot announcements. Multiple SaaS companies that raised Series B rounds in 2023 and 2024 are now struggling to close follow-on funding as investors redirect attention and capital toward AI infrastructure. The broader SaaS market has been under significant pressure throughout 2025 and 2026, with the sector experiencing notable stock declines as AI agents threaten to automate many traditional software workflows.
Climate tech presents a particularly stark contrast. Despite growing urgency around decarbonization, climate-focused startups raised less in Q1 2026 than in Q1 2024, according to multiple venture data providers. Energy transition companies that had attracted enthusiasm during the post-IRA boom of 2023-2024 are now competing for investor attention in a market where every conversation returns to AI.
Corporate Venture Capital: Big Tech Becomes Its Own VC
A defining feature of Q1 2026 was the scale of corporate venture capital participation. Amazon, Nvidia, Microsoft, and SoftBank collectively contributed over $140 billion to the quarter’s top rounds, blurring the line between strategic investment and traditional venture capital. Amazon’s $50 billion contribution to OpenAI alone exceeded the total assets under management of most dedicated venture capital firms.
The corporate involvement raises complex questions about market structure and competition. When the same companies that build cloud infrastructure, manufacture GPUs, and operate the platforms on which AI applications run also hold equity stakes in the leading AI labs, the potential for vertical integration and foreclosure is significant. Antitrust regulators in the United States and European Union have already signaled interest in examining these relationships. The broader $650 billion AI infrastructure buildout only amplifies these concerns.
Nvidia’s dual role is particularly notable. The company invested approximately $30 billion in OpenAI while simultaneously being OpenAI’s primary GPU supplier. The arrangement creates incentive alignment – Nvidia benefits from OpenAI’s growth through both equity appreciation and hardware sales – but it also raises questions about whether competing AI labs can access Nvidia’s latest chips on equal terms.
The IPO Pipeline: Will Returns Justify the Capital?
The main test of Q1 2026’s venture capital surge will be whether these investments generate returns through public market exits. The IPO pipeline for AI-related companies is the most crowded in market history. Databricks has filed for what analysts expect to be the largest enterprise software IPO ever . SpaceX has filed for a listing that could value the company at $1.75 trillion. Anthropic’s leadership has discussed IPO timing publicly.
However, the IPO market for venture-backed companies has been sluggish relative to the private funding environment. Companies are staying private longer, raising ever-larger rounds rather than subjecting themselves to public market scrutiny. The gap between private and public market valuations has widened considerably, raising the risk of “down-round IPOs” where companies list below their last private valuation.
“The private market has essentially become the new public market for these companies,” noted Jenny Lee, managing partner at GGV Capital, in comments at the ASU+GSV Summit. “They can raise $30 billion privately and avoid quarterly earnings pressure. The question is whether public investors will pay these prices when the time comes.”
Unicorn Board Value Rises $900 Billion in a Single Quarter
The Crunchbase Unicorn Board, which tracks private companies valued at $1 billion or more, saw its aggregate value increase by approximately $900 billion in Q1 2026. This single-quarter increase exceeds the total value of the unicorn board as recently as 2019, underscoring the velocity of value creation in the private market.
OpenAI’s valuation jump from approximately $157 billion in its October 2024 round to $852 billion in March 2026 – a 5.4x increase in 18 months – accounts for a substantial portion of this gain. Anthropic’s rise to $380 billion represents another massive contributor. The top five unicorns by valuation now account for more than half of the entire unicorn board’s value.
The concentration creates systemic risk in venture portfolios. Limited partners in major venture funds are increasingly exposed to a small number of high-conviction AI bets. If the AI revenue growth narrative falters, or if a major regulatory action constrains frontier AI development, the markdowns could cascade through institutional portfolios that have allocated heavily to venture capital over the past two years.
Five Predictions for Venture Capital Through the End of 2026
1. Full-year 2026 VC funding will exceed $800 billion. If Q2 through Q4 maintain even half of Q1’s pace, 2026 will shatter the full-year record by a factor of two or more. The pipeline of AI infrastructure deals suggests the mega-round trend will continue through at least the first half of the year.
2. At least three AI companies will IPO at valuations above $100 billion. Databricks, Anthropic, and at least one other AI infrastructure company are expected to pursue public listings in 2026. The SpaceX filing, while not purely an AI company, will test public market appetite for trillion-dollar tech IPOs.
3. Non-AI startup funding will continue to decline in real terms. The capital gravitational pull of AI is unlikely to reverse in 2026. Fintech, cons
body_clean:
Nadia Dubois
April 19, 2026
14 min read
Global venture capital investment hit $297 billion in the first quarter of 2026, obliterating every prior record and marking the most concentrated capital deployment in startup history. According to Crunchbase data published on April 1, four of the five largest venture rounds ever recorded closed in a single 90-day window, with frontier AI labs capturing nearly two-thirds of every dollar invested. The quarter reshaped the power dynamics of Silicon Valley, elevated sovereign wealth funds to kingmaker status, and left a growing number of non-AI startups struggling to compete for attention and capital.
The numbers are staggering by any measure. Investors poured capital into approximately 6,000 startups worldwide, up more than 150% from the $118 billion raised in Q4 2025, according to TechCrunch and Crunchbase reporting. The United States absorbed $250 billion of the total, representing 81% of global venture funding, up from 55% a year earlier.[1][2] The concentration raises fundamental questions about whether the venture capital industry is funding a genuine technological revolution or inflating the largest asset bubble in financial history.
Google · Preferred Sources
Don't miss new tech stories on Google
Add Tech Insider once in the Google app and our stories appear in your news suggestions.
Add Now
The Four Mega-Rounds That Rewrote Venture Capital History
The Q1 2026 record rests almost entirely on four extraordinary funding rounds that collectively totaled $188 billion, accounting for roughly 63% of all global venture investment in the quarter. OpenAI’s $122 billion raise, the largest private funding round in history, valued the company at $852 billion and attracted a consortium of corporate and institutional investors. Amazon contributed approximately $50 billion, Nvidia $30 billion, and SoftBank $30 billion, with additional participation from Microsoft, Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia Capital, Thrive Capital, Temasek, and BlackRock.
Anthropic followed with a $30 billion Series G round that pushed its valuation to $380 billion. Singapore’s GIC and Coatue Management led the round, with participation from D.E. Shaw, Founders Fund, Microsoft, Nvidia, and the Qatar Investment Authority. Elon Musk’s xAI closed a $20 billion Series E, with Andreessen Horowitz leading alongside 8VC, Lightspeed Venture Partners, and Shield Capital. Waymo secured $16 billion from a group that included Lightspeed, SoftBank, Amazon, Jeff Bezos, and Coatue.
“The first quarter of 2026 didn’t just set a record – it shattered every benchmark venture capital has ever known,” noted analysis from Intellizence . “For the first time in history, a single funding round exceeded the entire prior quarterly record for global startup investment.” The OpenAI round alone surpassed the total venture capital deployed in the entire United States during most quarters of 2024.
Top 10 Largest Startup Funding Rounds in Q1 2026
Company Amount Raised Valuation Sector Lead Investors
OpenAI $122 billion $852 billion Generative AI Amazon, Nvidia, SoftBank
Anthropic $30 billion $380 billion Enterprise / Safety AI GIC, Coatue
xAI $20 billion Not disclosed Frontier AI Andreessen Horowitz
Waymo $16 billion Not disclosed Autonomous Vehicles Lightspeed, SoftBank
Databricks $5 billion $62 billion Data Infrastructure Thrive Capital
Figure AI $3.2 billion+ Not disclosed Robotics / AI Multiple investors
Shield AI $2 billion $12.7 billion Defense AI U.S. defense investors
CoreWeave $1.1 billion Not disclosed AI Cloud Infrastructure Multiple investors
FluidStack $1 billion $18 billion Neocloud / GPU Anthropic-backed
World Labs $1 billion Not disclosed Spatial Intelligence Institutional investors
The table above illustrates a defining feature of Q1 2026: the near-total domination of AI and AI-adjacent companies in mega-round fundraising. Every company in the top 10 either builds foundation models, provides AI infrastructure, or deploys AI in physical systems like autonomous vehicles and military drones. Traditional software, fintech, and consumer technology startups were almost entirely absent from the billion-dollar round club.
AI Captures 81% of All Venture Capital Dollars
The most striking statistic from Q1 2026 is the degree to which artificial intelligence has consumed the venture capital market. According to analysis from Trending Topics EU , AI companies absorbed 81% of all venture funding deployed in the quarter, totaling more than $240 billion. This represents the highest concentration of capital in a single technology sector in venture capital history, surpassing the dot-com era’s peak concentration in internet companies during Q1 2000.
The AI funding surge breaks down into two distinct categories. Frontier AI labs – companies building large language models and foundation models – captured the largest share, led by OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI. AI infrastructure companies, including GPU cloud providers like CoreWeave and FluidStack, data platforms like Databricks, and chip makers like Cerebras Systems, absorbed a significant secondary share. Application-layer AI startups, those building products on top of foundation models, received comparatively modest funding.
“What we’re seeing is a capital structure that looks more like the oil industry in the early 20th century than traditional venture capital,” said Sarah Chen, managing partner at Sapphire Ventures, in an interview with TechCrunch. “A handful of infrastructure players are absorbing almost all the capital, and everyone else is building on top of their platforms. The question is whether the returns will justify the concentration.”
Geographic Concentration: The U.S. Absorbs 83% of Global VC
The geographic distribution of Q1 2026 venture capital reveals an increasingly lopsided global landscape. The United States attracted $250 billion of the $297 billion total, representing 81% of global venture investment, up sharply from 55% in Q1 2025 according to Crunchbase data.[1][2] This concentration is almost entirely driven by the mega-rounds at U.S.-headquartered AI companies, all of which are based in San Francisco or the broader Bay Area.
China attracted $16.1 billion in Q1 2026, maintaining its position as the second-largest venture market despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and export controls on advanced AI chips. The United Kingdom drew $7.4 billion, buoyed by autonomous driving startup Wayve and continued growth in London’s fintech sector. According to KPMG’s Venture Pulse Q1 2026 report, Asia as a whole saw $33.6 billion in funding, with at least two $1 billion-plus rounds closing outside the United States.
“We are watching the largest wealth creation event in human history unfold almost entirely in one country,” said Dr. Thomas Ramge, a technology analyst at the Bertelsmann Foundation, in comments to the Financial Times. “Europe’s regulatory approach has created stability but not the kind of risk appetite that produces $100 billion funding rounds.”
Late-Stage Funding Surges 205% as Mega-Deals Dominate
The stage breakdown of Q1 2026 venture funding reveals extreme concentration at the late-stage end of the spectrum. Late-stage rounds accounted for $246.6 billion, or roughly 82% of all venture capital deployed, representing a 205% year-over-year increase according to Capitaly VC and Crunchbase data. Of this, $235 billion came from just 158 rounds of $100 million or more, meaning that fewer than 3% of all venture deals accounted for more than 79% of all capital.
Seed-stage funding showed healthier dynamics, rising 31% year-over-year to $12 billion. However, the number of seed deals fell 30% to approximately 3,800, indicating that while investors are writing larger seed checks, they are funding fewer companies. The average seed round size increased accordingly, a trend that benefits well-connected founders with strong networks but makes it harder for first-time founders without Silicon Valley connections to raise initial capital.
“The venture market has essentially bifurcated,” said Mike Volpi, general partner at Index Ventures, in an interview with Bloomberg. “You have a handful of companies raising rounds that look more like sovereign debt issuances, and then you have everyone else competing for a shrinking pool of capital. The middle has been hollowed out.”
Q1 2026 Venture Funding vs. Historical Quarters
Quarter Global VC Funding YoY Change Deal Count AI Share U.S. Share
Q1 2026 $297 billion +150% ~6,000 81% 83%
Q4 2025 $118 billion +22% ~7,200 52% 71%
Q3 2025 $97 billion +18% ~7,500 48% 68%
Q2 2025 $91 billion +15% ~7,800 44% 66%
Q1 2025 $85 billion +12% ~8,100 40% 71%
Q1 2022 (prior peak) $144 billion – ~10,500 18% 55%
The table above highlights two critical trends. First, Q1 2026 more than doubled the previous quarterly record set in Q1 2022 during the zero-interest-rate venture boom. Second, the number of deals actually declined relative to 2022 levels, meaning the record was driven entirely by larger round sizes rather than broader startup ecosystem growth. This concentration pattern has significant implications for startup diversity and innovation breadth.
Sovereign Wealth Funds Emerge as AI Kingmakers
One of the most significant structural shifts in Q1 2026 was the rise of sovereign wealth funds as decisive players in frontier AI funding. Singapore’s GIC co-led Anthropic’s $30 billion round. Temasek, another Singaporean sovereign fund, participated in OpenAI’s $122 billion raise. The Qatar Investment Authority backed Anthropic. Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund and Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala Investment Company have both increased their AI allocations substantially throughout 2025 and into 2026.
The sovereign fund involvement reflects a fundamental shift in how frontier AI development is financed. Traditional venture capital firms, even the largest, lack the balance sheet capacity to write $10 billion or $30 billion checks. Sovereign wealth funds, with combined assets exceeding $12 trillion globally, can deploy capital at this scale without the liquidity constraints that limit pension funds and university endowments.
“Sovereign wealth funds are now the most important investors in the AI ecosystem,” said Aswath Damodaran, professor of finance at New York University’s Stern School of Business. “They bring patient capital, geopolitical motivations, and balance sheets that dwarf anything a traditional VC firm can muster. The question is whether their involvement adds governance value or simply inflates valuations.”
The Valuation Question: $852 Billion for a Company With $11 Billion in Revenue
The most contentious aspect of Q1 2026’s venture capital surge is the valuation multiples being assigned to frontier AI companies. OpenAI’s $852 billion valuation comes on approximately $11.6 billion in annualized revenue, implying a revenue multiple of roughly 73x. Anthropic’s $380 billion valuation on significantly lower revenue implies an even higher multiple. By comparison, the most richly valued public technology companies typically trade at 15-25x revenue.
Defenders of these valuations argue that frontier AI companies are building infrastructure that will underpin the entire economy, similar to how cloud computing platforms generated returns that justified early investments in AWS and Azure. The bull case rests on the assumption that AI will capture a significant share of global GDP, currently estimated at $105 trillion, making even an $852 billion valuation modest relative to the total addressable market.
Critics counter that the valuations reflect greater-fool dynamics rather than fundamental analysis. “We have never seen this level of capital concentration in pre-profit companies in any industry, ever,” said Scott Galloway, professor of marketing at NYU Stern. “The implicit assumption is that these companies will achieve margins and scale that exceed anything in economic history. That may happen, but the price already reflects a perfect outcome.”
Non-AI Startups Face a Capital Drought
While AI companies bathed in record capital, the rest of the startup ecosystem experienced what founders are calling a “capital drought.” With 81% of all venture dollars flowing to AI, the remaining $57 billion was split among thousands of fintech, biotech, climate tech, enterprise SaaS, and consumer startups. The broader SaaS market has been under significant pressure throughout 2025 and 2026, with the sector experiencing notable stock declines as AI agents threaten to automate many traditional software workflows.

Energy transition companies that had attracted enthusiasm during the post-IRA boom of 2023-2024 are now competing for investor attention in a market where every conversation returns to AI.
